metadata.summary: Comprehensive guide to Django code quality covering linting, formatting, security, performance optimization, logging, and system checks for production-grade applications.

content.title: Django Code Quality
content.summary: Django code quality encompasses multiple dimensions: code style, type safety, security, performance optimization, observability, and architecture. This methodology covers linting with Ruff, static typing with mypy and django-stubs, secure coding patterns, N+1 query prevention, structured logging, and custom system checks.

why.p: Quality is not ornamental. High-quality Django code costs less to maintain, ships faster, and survives refactoring. Ruff catches import mistakes and deprecated patterns faster than code review. Type hints via mypy + django-stubs catch attribute errors before they reach production. Query optimization (select_related, prefetch_related) is the difference between 200ms and 20s response times. Structured logging lets production teams diagnose real incidents. Custom system checks catch configuration errors on deploy before they break users.

The "Simple Service Layer" pattern (Hacksoft style) unifies code paths: admin actions, Celery tasks, management commands, and views all call the same business logic functions, reducing bugs and making tests meaningful. Security baseline checks (CSP, HSTS, CSRF, rate limiting) are not optional in production.

list.item: Any Django project shipping to production
Teams of two or more developers (code review becomes mandatory)
Codebases expected to survive six months or longer
Projects handling sensitive data or user authentication
Legacy projects where team consensus favors minimal change

section.name: code-quality-stack
section.p: 2025-2026 Django quality tools have consolidated into a lean stack. Ruff replaces Flake8/isort/Black. mypy with django-stubs provides type safety. pre-commit gates all commits with linting and type checks.

rule.statement: Every Django project MUST have: (1) Ruff configured in pyproject.toml for linting + formatting; (2) mypy + django-stubs for static type checking; (3) .pre-commit-config.yaml hooking ruff and mypy; (4) GitHub Actions or CI running pytest with coverage threshold (≥80%). Configuration MUST be committed to the repo and applied automatically, not manually by developers.
rule.rationale: Manual code review catches logical errors; tools catch mechanical mistakes faster. Ruff is 50x faster than chained Flake8/isort/Black. Pre-commit gates prevent bad code entering the repo. Type hints + mypy catch attribute/import errors days before runtime. CI coverage thresholds enforce test discipline over time.

example.title: Ruff + mypy configuration
example.code:
# pyproject.toml [tool.ruff] line-length = 100 target-version = "py312" select = ["E", "W", "F", "I", "B", "C4", "UP", "SIM", "DJ"] exclude = ["migrations", "venv"]
[tool.ruff.isort] force-single-line = true
[tool.mypy] python_version = "3.12" plugins = ["mypy_django_plugin.main"] check_untyped_defs = true warn_unused_ignores = true
[tool.django-stubs] django_settings_module = "config.settings"

example.title: pre-commit hooks
example.code:
# .pre-commit-config.yaml repos: - repo: https://github.com/astral-sh/ruff-pre-commit rev: v0.6.0 hooks: - id: ruff args: [--fix] - id: ruff-format
- repo: https://github.com/pre-commit/mirrors-mypy rev: v1.13.0 hooks: - id: mypy additional_dependencies: [django-stubs, types-requests]

section.name: service-layer-pattern
section.p: The "Simple Service Layer" (Hacksoft Django Styleguide) centralizes business logic in functions (not classes). All code paths (views, admin, Celery, management commands) call the same service functions, preventing divergent bugs.

rule.statement: Business logic MUST live in services.py, not views.py or models.py. Service functions are: (1) stateless, (2) take keyword-only arguments, (3) have full type hints, (4) transaction-wrapped where needed, (5) raise specific exceptions (not generic Exception). Views/admin/tasks call services; services call models and other services. This unifies code paths and makes testing straightforward.
rule.rationale: Services decouple interface (HTTP, admin, CLI) from business logic (order creation, payment, reporting). One bug fix in the service fixes all callers. Testing a service is cleaner than testing through views. Transaction handling is centralized, not scattered across views.

example.title: Service layer with transaction handling
example.code:
# services.py from django.db import transaction from django.core.exceptions import ValidationError from typing import Optional import logging
from .models import Order, OrderItem, Product
logger = logging.getLogger(__name__)
def create_order( *, user: User, items: list[dict], shipping_address_id: int, notes: Optional[str] = None ) -> Order: """ Create a new order with items.
Raises: ValidationError: If items are invalid or out of stock """ _validate_order_items(items)
with transaction.atomic(): order = Order.objects.create( user=user, shipping_address_id=shipping_address_id, notes=notes or "" ) _create_order_items(order=order, items=items) _update_inventory(items=items) logger.info("Order created", extra={"order_id": order.id})
# Async task outside transaction send_order_confirmation.delay(order_id=order.id) return order
def _validate_order_items(items: list[dict]) -> None: """Validate items exist and have stock.""" if not items: raise ValidationError("Order must have at least one item") # ... validation logic

section.name: query-optimization
section.p: N+1 queries are the most common performance leak in Django. A single unapt for loop over orders becomes 1 + N database calls. select_related and prefetch_related eliminate this.

rule.statement: Rule: Every QuerySet that will be iterated MUST use select_related() for ForeignKey/OneToOne or prefetch_related() for ManyToMany/reverse FK BEFORE iteration. Use Django Debug Toolbar (dev) or django-silk (production) to count queries. One query per view is impossible; target: list endpoints ≤5 queries, detail endpoints ≤10 queries. Indexes on frequently filtered fields are non-negotiable.
rule.rationale: N+1 is invisible until you exceed 100 rows. select_related adds a JOIN (same query cost); prefetch_related is 2 queries (much cheaper than 1+N). Debug Toolbar makes the problem visible during development. Indexes are one-time setup, orders of magnitude cheaper than query optimization.

example.title: Query optimization patterns
example.code:
from django.db.models import Prefetch from django.utils import timezone from datetime import timedelta
# Problem: N+1 queries orders = Order.objects.all() for order in orders: print(order.user.email) # Query per order!
# Solution 1: select_related for ForeignKey orders = Order.objects.select_related("user").all()
# Solution 2: prefetch_related for ManyToMany orders = Order.objects.prefetch_related("items").all() for order in orders: for item in order.items.all(): # No extra query print(item.name)
# Solution 3: Combined + nested prefetch orders = Order.objects.select_related("user").prefetch_related( "items", "items__product" # Nested prefetch ).all()
# Solution 4: Custom Prefetch for filtered related recent_orders = Prefetch( "orders", queryset=Order.objects.filter( created_at__gte=timezone.now() - timedelta(days=30) ), to_attr="recent_orders" # Access via user.recent_orders ) users = User.objects.prefetch_related(recent_orders)
# Solution 5: only/defer for large fields articles = Article.objects.defer("content", "raw_html")

section.name: security-baseline
section.p: Security baseline is not optional. HTTPS, HSTS, CSRF, CSP, rate limiting, and secure password handling are the minimum for production.

rule.statement: Production Django MUST: (1) Set DEBUG=False; (2) Enable HTTPS with SECURE_SSL_REDIRECT=True; (3) Set HSTS headers (SECURE_HSTS_SECONDS=31536000); (4) Set X-Frame-Options to DENY; (5) Enable CSRF middleware; (6) Use secure session/CSRF cookies; (7) Rate limit public endpoints; (8) Validate all user input via forms/serializers; (9) Run python manage.py check --deploy before deploy; (10) Log failed authentication attempts.
rule.rationale: Each setting prevents a class of attacks: HTTPS prevents credential sniffing, HSTS prevents downgrade attacks, CSRF cookies prevent cross-origin form hijacking, rate limiting prevents brute force. check --deploy catches misconfigurations. These are not negotiable; they are the baseline.

example.title: Production security settings
example.code:
# settings/production.py DEBUG = False
# HTTPS SECURE_SSL_REDIRECT = True SESSION_COOKIE_SECURE = True CSRF_COOKIE_SECURE = True
# Security headers SECURE_BROWSER_XSS_FILTER = True SECURE_CONTENT_TYPE_NOSNIFF = True X_FRAME_OPTIONS = "DENY" SECURE_HSTS_SECONDS = 31536000 # 1 year SECURE_HSTS_INCLUDE_SUBDOMAINS = True SECURE_HSTS_PRELOAD = True
# CSP (Django 6.0+) CONTENT_SECURITY_POLICY = { "default-src": ["'self'"], "script-src": ["'self'", "'nonce-{}'"], "style-src": ["'self'"], "img-src": ["'self'", "data:"], }
# Before deploy # python manage.py check --deploy

section.name: exception-handling
section.p: Bare except: statements hide bugs. Specific exception handling documents intent and makes debugging faster.

rule.statement: NEVER use bare except: or except Exception:. Always catch specific exceptions (User.DoesNotExist, ValidationError, Timeout, etc.). Log the exception with logger.error(..., exc_info=True) to include the traceback. Re-raise or return meaningful errors, never silently pass.
rule.rationale: Bare except hides programming errors (AttributeError, KeyError, etc.) that should never happen. Specific exceptions document the failure mode. exc_info=True includes the traceback in logs, critical for debugging. Silent failures make bugs invisible until they cause downstream corruption.

example.title: Specific exception handling

section.name: type-hints
section.p: Type hints are documentation that is checked at commit time. With django-stubs, mypy catches attribute and method errors before runtime.

rule.statement: All functions and methods MUST have type hints on parameters and return values. Use django-stubs types (e.g., QuerySet[User] instead of generic QuerySet). Run mypy check_untyped_defs=true in CI. # type: ignore comments MUST include a reason explaining why the type error is acceptable.
rule.rationale: Type hints make code self-documenting. mypy catches typos like order.user_id (correct) vs order.user.id (causes AttributeError on None user). django-stubs types make Django ORM operations type-safe. CI enforcement prevents type degradation.

example.title: Type-hinted Django code

section.name: logging
section.p: Structured logging (JSON) is essential for production debugging. Logging at key points (service entry/exit, errors, state changes) makes incidents traceable.

rule.statement: Use structlog or django-structlog for JSON structured logging in production. Log at INFO level: important business events (order created, payment processed, user registered). Log at ERROR level: exceptions that require human action. Log at WARNING level: recoverable errors (rate limit hit, invalid input). Never log passwords, tokens, or PII. Include request_id or trace_id for distributed tracing.
rule.rationale: JSON logs are machine-readable, enabling alerts and queries in Datadog/Sentry. Structured logging (not freeform strings) lets ops teams search log volume. Tracing IDs link related events across services. Bare log messages are noise; contextualized events are signals.

example.title: Structured logging with django-structlog
example.code:
import structlog
logger = structlog.get_logger(__name__)
def process_payment(order_id: int, amount: Decimal) -> bool: """Process payment with structured logging.""" log = logger.bind(order_id=order_id, amount=str(amount))
log.info("payment_processing_started")
try: result = payment_gateway.charge(amount) log.info( "payment_processed", transaction_id=result.transaction_id, status=result.status ) return True except PaymentError as e: log.error( "payment_failed", error_code=e.code, error_message=str(e), exc_info=True # Include traceback ) return False

section.name: system-checks
section.p: Custom system checks catch configuration errors on deploy, before they reach users. Django's check system runs on startup and in CI.

rule.statement: Register custom checks via @register decorator for configuration that affects multiple modules (e.g., SECRET_KEY safety, Sentry setup, cache backend). Each check returns a list of Error/Warning objects with (1) message, (2) hint, (3) id for filtering.
rule.rationale: System checks are cheap safety nets. A check that detects "SECRET_KEY is still default" prevents credential exposure. A check for missing SENTRY_DSN catches monitoring gaps. Checks run every startup, catching configuration drift.

example.title: Custom system checks

template.purpose: Ruff, mypy, pytest configuration in pyproject.toml
Custom system checks for configuration validation
